test_id_1.query_content: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

policy.text:
Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx
the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, and Medicaid
Update articles. All NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims (when NYS Medicaid is primary payor)
must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost. Providers should refer to the
NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, for requirements
when NYS Medicaid is secondary payor. Claim level identifiers are required on eligible 340B purchased drug
claims for NYS Medicaid members.
Through monitoring real-time claims, NYRx has identified two trends in pharmacy submitted 340B
drug claims:
1.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
January 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Providers should refer to the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy
Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guide
lines.pdf, for further guidance on origin code and serial number values that must be submitted on the claim
for “pharmacy dispensing” when applicable for non-patient specific orders. Enter a value of “5” in field 419-
DJ (Prescription Origin Code) and a value of “99999999” in field 454- EK (Scheduled Prescription ID
Number). Additionally, providers should refer to the NYS Department of Health (DOH) Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Standard Companion Guide – Transaction Information, located at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
Reminder for Pharmacy and Medical Claims
The National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering provider is required on the NYS Medicaid claim.
Additionally, providers must not bill for the provision of the RSV vaccine to NYS Medicaid members who are
also enrolled in Medicare.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update from February 1, 2024, and should therefore receive more weight regarding guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims. The provider manual specifies that Medicaid enrolled pharmacies must submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services for dual eligible members, with payment not exceeding the Medicaid-only fee, while policy documents primarily refer to general pharmacy billing guidelines or specific immunization services for dual eligibles.
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents indicate that for dual eligible members, pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members for immunization services, with dual eligible members continuing to access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 01, 2021]. For general pharmacy claims, providers are directed to refer to the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — April 01, 2023]. Policy also states that pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims as outlined in the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines and Medicaid Update articles, requiring NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims (when NYS Medicaid is primary payor) to be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — February 01, 2024].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are federally required to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — October 01, 2025]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider for these services will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made, and the combined Medicare and Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. Pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources implicitly acknowledge the existence of dual eligible members and the interaction between Medicare and Medicaid for their services [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 01, 2021], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — October 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly details the federal requirement for Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services for dual eligible members, and outlines payment limitations (not exceeding Medicaid-only fee) and the prohibition against billing for Medicare Part B deductibles/coinsurance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — October 01, 2025].

Policy documents, particularly the August 2021 update, specifically address immunization services for dual eligible members, stating that they continue to access these services through Medicare, and pharmacies can only bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members for immunizations [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 01, 2021].

Policy documents frequently refer providers to other comprehensive billing guidelines or policy manuals for general pharmacy claims or specific drug programs like 340B, rather than providing direct guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims within the update articles themselves [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — April 01, 2023], [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — February 01, 2024].

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy guidance from February 1, 2024, and should therefore receive more weight regarding dual eligible pharmacy claims. Both sources emphasize that Medicaid is the payer of last resort for dual eligible members, requiring Medicare to be billed first.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source dated February 1, 2023, Medicaid is the payer of last resort for dual eligible enrollees, meaning all other reimbursement sources, including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid, as per federal law [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 - February 1, 2023]. Earlier policy guidance from August 1, 2021, stated that pharmacies could only bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members for immunization services, with dual eligible members accessing these services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 - August 1, 2021].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are federally required to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of their CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 - October 1, 2025]. The NYS Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies to ensure dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and state laws and regulations [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 - October 1, 2025]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 - October 1, 2025]. Medicare and Medicaid payments must be accepted as payment in full, and providers cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 - October 1, 2025].
similarities: Both sources indicate that Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort for dual eligible members, requiring Medicare to be billed first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 - February 1, 2023] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 - October 1, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services for dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 - October 1, 2025], a detail not present in the provided policy excerpts.

The provider manual specifies that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee, and no additional payment will be made if Medicare payment is higher, with both payments accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 - October 1, 2025]. This level of detail regarding payment caps and acceptance as payment in full is not found in the provided policy documents.

An older policy document from August 1, 2021, specifically stated that pharmacies could only bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members for immunization services, with dual eligible members accessing these services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 - August 1, 2021], which is a specific service-related constraint not reiterated in the newer policy or the provider manual excerpts.

amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, provides more recent and detailed guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims compared to the latest policy updates from April 2023. The provider manual should receive more weight by default for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source dated April 1, 2023, for pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy and page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — April 1, 2023]. Earlier policy guidance from February 1, 2023, states that Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring potential third-party reimbursement sources, including Medicare, to be billed prior to Medicaid for dual eligible enrollees [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 1, 2023]. For immunization services, dual eligible members continue to access services through Medicare, and pharmacies can only bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 1, 2021].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are federally required to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — October 1, 2025]. The NYS Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to ensure dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and State laws and regulations [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — October 1, 2025]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025]. Pharmacy providers cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025].
similarities: Both sources indicate that Medicare is the primary payer for dual eligible members, with Medicaid acting as the payer of last resort [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 1, 2023] and requiring pharmacies to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — October 1, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee and that no additional payment will be made if Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, a detail not specified in the provided policy documents [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025].

The provider manual details the requirement for Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to serve dual eligible members, including different types of Medicare enrollment (CMS-460, CMS-855B, CMS-855S), which is not elaborated upon in the provided policy excerpts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — October 1, 2025].

Policy documents specifically mention that pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members for immunization services, with dual eligible members accessing these services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 1, 2021], a specific service carve-out not present in the provided provider manual excerpts.

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.

• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, provides more recent and detailed guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims compared to the latest policy update from January 1, 2024. Therefore, the provider manual should receive more weight for current guidance on this topic.
answers.policy_definition: For dual eligible enrollees, Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort, meaning all other reimbursement sources, including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid, as per federal law [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023]. Pharmacies were previously instructed to bill only for Medicaid non-dual eligible members for immunization services, with dual eligible members accessing these services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 01, 2021]. Providers should refer to the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines for further guidance on claim submission details [New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1:7, 8 — January 01, 2024].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies to ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and State laws and regulations [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — October 01, 2025]. All Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are federally required to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of their CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — October 01, 2025]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the provider would receive for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. Pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources indicate that Medicaid is the payer of last resort for dual eligible members, requiring Medicare to be billed first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023] and that pharmacies must submit claims for Medicare covered services for dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — October 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that the NYS Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies to serve dual eligible members and outlines the federal requirement for all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services for dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — October 01, 2025]. The policy documents, while mentioning Medicaid as the payer of last resort, do not detail the specific Medicare enrollment requirements for pharmacies or the acceptance of assignment for Medicare covered services for dual eligibles.

The provider manual specifies that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only rate and that no additional payment is made if Medicare payment exceeds the Medicaid fee, also prohibiting recovery of Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance from dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. The policy documents do not provide these specific payment limitations or prohibitions on cost recovery from dual eligible members.

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy updates from February 2024 and should receive more weight for current guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims. The provider manual specifies requirements for Medicare enrollment for pharmacies serving dual eligible members and outlines payment limitations, while policy documents primarily refer to general pharmacy billing guidelines and 340B claims without specific details on dual eligible pharmacy claims.
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents primarily direct providers to general pharmacy billing guidelines and policy manuals for claims. For instance, pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims as outlined in the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines and Medicaid Update articles [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 - February 1, 2024]. All NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims (when NYS Medicaid is primary payor) must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 - February 1, 2024]. For general pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 - April 1, 2023]. An older policy update from August 2021 stated that pharmacies would only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members, and dual eligible members would continue to access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 - August 1, 2021].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 - October 1, 2025]. The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to ensure dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and State laws and regulations [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 - October 1, 2025]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 - October 1, 2025]. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made, and the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 - October 1, 2025]. Pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 - October 1, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly details the requirement for Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services for dual eligible members, and the necessity of Medicare enrollment for pharmacies serving these members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 - October 1, 2025]. Policy documents, particularly the newest ones, do not provide specific guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims, instead referring to general pharmacy billing guidelines or 340B claims [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 - February 1, 2024].

The provider manual outlines payment limitations for dual eligible claims, stating that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee and that providers cannot recover Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 - October 1, 2025]. Policy documents do not contain these specific payment limitations for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

An older policy update from August 2021 indicated that pharmacies would only bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members for immunization services, with dual eligible members accessing services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 - August 1, 2021]. The provider manual, being more recent, mandates that Medicaid enrolled pharmacies are required to submit claims for Medicare covered services for dual eligible members, suggesting a broader scope than just immunizations and a direct billing requirement for pharmacies [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 - October 1, 2025].

test_id_6.query_content: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_6.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update from April 1, 2025, and provides more detailed guidance on primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, specifically stating that Medicare benefits must be maximized before billing Medicaid. Both sources emphasize the provider's responsibility to ensure proper billing to the primary payer.
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents indicate that certain services may be rejected by the primary payer, but coverage should still be pursued, including situations where prior authorization is needed from the primary payer or when billing Medicare Part D for services covered by Part B, or vice versa, and receiving a rejection [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:8 — 2025-04-01]. It is the responsibility of providers, including pharmacies, to actively and regularly seek Third Party Liability (TPL) enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients, and to bill timely and to the appropriate party, such as a commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01]. Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs excluded from TPL, including Medicare, through coordination of benefits [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01]. For pharmacy claims, providers are directed to refer to the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy and the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, Medicare benefits must be maximized prior to billing Medicaid for services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full, and a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. It is the provider's responsibility to determine if the patient is covered by other insurance and if that insurance covers the billed service [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].
similarities: Both sources emphasize the provider's responsibility to ensure proper billing to the primary payer [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].

Both sources acknowledge that Medicaid acts as a secondary payer, covering patient responsibility or allowed amounts after the primary payer [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that Medicare benefits must be maximized prior to billing Medicaid for services covered by both [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01], a detail not as directly stated in the provided policy excerpts.

The provider manual specifies that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only rate and that Medicare/Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full, prohibiting recovery of Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents do not contain these specific payment limitations.

The policy documents provide examples of situations where coverage should still be pursued even after primary payer rejection, such as needing prior authorization or incorrect billing between Medicare Part B and D [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:8 — 2025-04-01], which are not detailed in the provided provider manual excerpts.

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

***Enrollment in this Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries one or more items that would be required to be billed

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update from April 1, 2025, and provides more detailed guidance on primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims. Both sources agree that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and other insurance, including Medicare, must be billed first.
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, meaning all other available resources, including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid for dual-eligible enrollees [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. This federal requirement ensures that Medicaid only makes payments after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. Providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: For dual-eligible members, enrollment in a Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries items that would be billed to Medicare on behalf of the member [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:23 — 2025-10-01]. Providers must determine if a patient is covered by other insurance and if that carrier covers the service being billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23]. If Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made, and the combined Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources establish that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and requires other potential third-party reimbursement sources, such as Medicare, to be billed first for dual-eligible pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01], [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01].

Both imply the provider's responsibility to identify and bill the appropriate primary payer [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].
differences: The provider manual specifies that if the Medicare payment exceeds the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made, and the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full, a detail not explicitly covered in the policy documents [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members, which is not detailed in the policy excerpts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual indicates that enrollment in a Medicare type is required for pharmacies carrying items billable to Medicare for dual-eligible members, a specific requirement not found in the policy documents [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:23 — 2025-10-01].

Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
***Enrollment in this Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries one or more items that would be required to be billed
to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update from April 1, 2025, and should receive more weight. Both sources consistently define Medicaid as the payer of last resort for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, requiring other liable third parties, including Medicare, to be billed first.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source, the New York State Medicaid Update April 2025, NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. Earlier policy guidance from February 2023 also states that Medicaid is the payer of last resort, meaning payments are made only after all other reimbursement sources, including Medicare, have been exhausted and must be billed prior to Medicaid [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source, the New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made, and the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Providers are responsible for determining if a patient is covered by other insurance and if that carrier covers the billed service [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23]. Enrollment in Medicare is required when the pharmacy carries items that would be billed to Medicare for a dual-eligible member [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:23 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both policy and provider manual guidance establish that Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring other liable third parties, such as Medicare, to be billed first for dual-eligible pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]; [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual specifies that the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee, and if Medicare payment is higher, no additional Medicaid payment will be made, which is not explicitly detailed in the provided policy excerpts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that enrollment in Medicare is required for pharmacies carrying items billable to Medicare for dual-eligible members, a detail not found in the provided policy documents [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:23 — 2025-10-01].

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update from April 1, 2025, and provides more detailed guidance on primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims. Both sources agree that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and other insurance, including Medicare, must be billed first.
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, meaning all other available resources, including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid for dual-eligible enrollees [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. Providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. This aligns with federal law stating Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: For dual-eligible members, enrollment in a Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries items that would be billed to Medicare on behalf of such a member [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:23 — 2025-10-01]. Providers are responsible for determining if a patient has other insurance and if that carrier covers the service being billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would receive for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full, and a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources establish that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and requires other insurance, including Medicare, to be billed first for dual-eligible pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01], [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].
differences: The provider manual specifies that enrollment in a Medicare type is required for pharmacies carrying items billable to Medicare for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:23 — 2025-10-01], a detail not explicitly stated in the policy documents.

The provider manual provides specific guidance on payment limitations, stating that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee and that Medicare/Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full, prohibiting recovery of Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents do not elaborate on these payment specifics.

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
May 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
October 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Policy and Billing
Reminder to all Providers on Billing Requirements for
Medicare Part C Claims That are Submitted to Medicaid for
Payment of Patient Responsibility
Reminder: All providers must accurately report Medicare Part C cost-sharing amounts on Medicaid
claims. Some providers may be inflating the patient responsibility amount for Part C Medicare Advantage claims,
resulting in overpayment by the Medicaid program and therefore may be leaving the providers subject to
investigation by the Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG).
A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot seek to recover any copayment, or coinsurance amount
from Medicare/Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare Part C
health plan payment and any Medicaid payment as payment in full for the service. The member may not be billed
for any Medicare Part C copayment/coinsurance amount that was not reimbursed by Medicaid.
