policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Refusing to
provide services is an unacceptable practice.
o Providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through methods such as requesting the
copayment each time the member is provided with services or goods, sending bills or any other
legal means.
• Pharmacy providers must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is
collected from a Medicaid member. Each claim that requires a co-payment will have the co-payment
automatically deducted from the final payment when the claim is approved for payment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medical and Surgical Supplies
Reimbursement for each covered medical/surgical supply will be the lower of:
• The price as indicated on the New York State List of Medical/Surgical Supplies, or
• The usual and customary price charged to the general public.
"Covered supplies" are those on the OTC and Supply Fee Schedule manual located here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/index.aspx. For supplies not on this list, only those supplies for
which the prescriber has obtained prior approval are covered.
Co-payments for Drugs and Medical Supplies
The New York State Medicaid Program charges co-payments for many drug and medical supply items.
Health care providers have an obligation to provide services and goods regardless of a Medicaid member's ability to
pay co-payments:
• Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to
pay the co-payment, per Social Security Act (SSA) § 1916 and Title 18 NYCRR § 360-7.12.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update from May 1, 2024, and provides more detailed guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members, particularly for pharmacy providers. The provider manual should receive more weight by default for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: Providers of a Medicare Part C benefit are prohibited from attempting to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare payment [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024]. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, meaning all other sources, including Medicare, must be billed prior to Medicaid [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023]. Providers must accurately report Medicare Part C cost-sharing amounts on Medicaid claims [New York State Medicaid Update October 2019 Volume 35 Number 10:5, 6 — October 01, 2019].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare and Medicaid dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. The combined Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full, and the total payment will not exceed what the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. Providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through legal means, such as requesting payment each time services are provided or sending bills [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — October 01, 2025]. However, pharmacy providers must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment collected from a Medicaid member, as the copayment will be automatically deducted from the final payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — October 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources indicate that providers cannot recover certain cost-sharing amounts directly from dual-eligible members. The policy states that providers of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover co-payment or co-insurance from dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024]. Similarly, the provider manual states that pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].
differences: The policy document specifically addresses Medicare Part C benefits, stating that providers cannot recover co-payment or co-insurance from dual-eligible individuals for these benefits [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024]. In contrast, the provider manual focuses on pharmacy providers and Medicare Part B benefits, prohibiting them from recovering Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].

The provider manual provides more detailed guidance on the collection process for copayments, stating that providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through legal means, but also clarifies that pharmacy providers must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the collected copayment, as it will be automatically deducted [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — October 01, 2025]. The policy documents do not offer this level of detail regarding the collection process or the impact on Medicaid claims.

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
May 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
October 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Policy and Billing
Reminder to all Providers on Billing Requirements for
Medicare Part C Claims That are Submitted to Medicaid for
Payment of Patient Responsibility
Reminder: All providers must accurately report Medicare Part C cost-sharing amounts on Medicaid
claims. Some providers may be inflating the patient responsibility amount for Part C Medicare Advantage claims,
resulting in overpayment by the Medicaid program and therefore may be leaving the providers subject to
investigation by the Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG).
A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot seek to recover any copayment, or coinsurance amount
from Medicare/Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare Part C
health plan payment and any Medicaid payment as payment in full for the service. The member may not be billed
for any Medicare Part C copayment/coinsurance amount that was not reimbursed by Medicaid.

.pdf.

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Since co-payments paid or incurred can be used toward satisfying the spend-down (overage)
in the following month, itemized bills or receipts for co-payments should be provided to members when
requested.
• There is a maximum amount per Medicaid member for all co-payments incurred per year and is calculated
on a quarterly basis. The co-payment year starts April 1 and ends March 31. When a member reaches the
quarterly co-pay maximum, they will receive a letter confirming the date on which the co-pay maximum
was met and exempting the member from a co-payment until the end of the current co-payment quarter.
• Co-payment amounts are as follows:
o $3.00 for non-preferred Brand Name Drugs.
o $1.00 for Generic Drugs, preferred Brand Name Drugs, and Brand Drugs included in the Brand Less
Than Generic Drugs Program.
o $0.50 for Non-Prescription (over the counter) Drugs.
o $1.00 for Medical/Sickroom Supplies.
• Co-payment is not required for certain members and service categories which include:

o Family planning (birth control) services including birth control pills, emergency contraception, and
condoms.

Both sources indicate that providers cannot seek co-payments directly from dual-eligible members, but the provider manual specifically addresses Medicare Part B, while policy documents address Medicare Part C.
answers.policy_definition: Providers of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare payment as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 - May 1, 2024]. answers.provider_manual_definition: A pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare and Medicaid dually eligible individuals. Co-payments paid or incurred can be used toward satisfying the spend-down in the following month, and itemized bills or receipts for co-payments should be provided to members when requested [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40, 41 - October 1, 2025]. There is a maximum annual co-payment amount per Medicaid member, calculated quarterly, with the co-payment year starting April 1 and ending March 31 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40, 41 - October 1, 2025].
similarities: Both policy and provider manual documents state that providers cannot seek to recover co-payment or co-insurance amounts directly from dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 - May 1, 2024]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 - October 1, 2025].

differences: The policy documents specifically refer to co-payment responsibility for Medicare Part C benefits, stating that providers cannot recover co-payments from dual-eligible individuals for these benefits [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 - May 1, 2024].

The provider manual specifically refers to co-payment responsibility for Medicare Part B benefits, stating that pharmacy providers cannot seek to recover Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 - October 1, 2025].

The provider manual includes details about co-payments contributing to a member's spend-down and an annual co-payment maximum, which is calculated quarterly [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40, 41 - October 1, 2025]. These details are not present in the provided policy excerpts.

policy.text:
October 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Policy and Billing
Reminder to all Providers on Billing Requirements for
Medicare Part C Claims That are Submitted to Medicaid for
Payment of Patient Responsibility
Reminder: All providers must accurately report Medicare Part C cost-sharing amounts on Medicaid
claims. Some providers may be inflating the patient responsibility amount for Part C Medicare Advantage claims,
resulting in overpayment by the Medicaid program and therefore may be leaving the providers subject to
investigation by the Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG).
A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot seek to recover any copayment, or coinsurance amount
from Medicare/Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare Part C
health plan payment and any Medicaid payment as payment in full for the service. The member may not be billed
for any Medicare Part C copayment/coinsurance amount that was not reimbursed by Medicaid.

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:

_mmc.pdf.

guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.

The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

provider_manual.text:
Medical and Surgical Supplies
Reimbursement for each covered medical/surgical supply will be the lower of:
• The price as indicated on the New York State List of Medical/Surgical Supplies, or
• The usual and customary price charged to the general public.
"Covered supplies" are those on the OTC and Supply Fee Schedule manual located here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/index.aspx. For supplies not on this list, only those supplies for
which the prescriber has obtained prior approval are covered.
Co-payments for Drugs and Medical Supplies
The New York State Medicaid Program charges co-payments for many drug and medical supply items.
Health care providers have an obligation to provide services and goods regardless of a Medicaid member's ability to
pay co-payments:
• Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to
pay the co-payment, per Social Security Act (SSA) § 1916 and Title 18 NYCRR § 360-7.12.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, which is effective May 1, 2024. Both sources indicate that providers cannot recover co-payments directly from dual-eligible individuals, but the provider manual also details how pharmacies should handle co-payments for Medicaid-only patients, which is not addressed in the policy documents provided.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source dated May 1, 2024, a provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare payment [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 1, 2024]. Earlier policy guidance from October 1, 2019, also reminded providers to accurately report Medicare Part C cost-sharing amounts on Medicaid claims and noted that inflating patient responsibility amounts could lead to overpayment by Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update October 2019 Volume 35 Number 10:5, 6 — October 1, 2019]. Medicaid is the payer of last resort and must be billed only after all other sources, including Medicare, have been exhausted [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 1, 2023].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare and Medicaid dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025]. The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025]. For Medicaid-only patients, providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through methods such as requesting the copayment each time services are provided or sending bills. However, pharmacy providers must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment collected from a Medicaid member, as each claim requiring a co-payment will have it automatically deducted from the final payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — October 1, 2025].
similarities: Both the policy documents and the provider manual state that providers cannot recover co-payment or co-insurance amounts directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 1, 2024]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025].
differences: The provider manual specifies that for Medicaid-only patients, providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through various legal means, but must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the collected copayment, as it will be automatically deducted [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — October 1, 2025]. The policy documents do not provide specific guidance on co-payment collection methods for Medicaid-only patients.

The policy documents explicitly mention Medicare Part C benefits and the responsibility of providers to report Medicare Advantage Plan deductible, co-insurance, co-payment, and paid amounts on claims [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 1, 2024]. The provider manual, in the provided excerpts, specifically refers to Medicare Part B benefits for pharmacy providers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025].

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update from May 1, 2024, and provides more detailed guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members, particularly concerning pharmacy services. The provider manual should receive more weight by default for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source dated May 1, 2024, a provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare payment as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 1, 2024]. Earlier policy guidance from February 1, 2023, states that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and other reimbursement sources, including Medicare, must be billed prior to Medicaid [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 1, 2023].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare and Medicaid dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025]. The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full, and the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the amount the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025]. For Medicare Part D members, Medicaid only pays for drugs specifically excluded by law from Part D plans, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 1, 2025]. Providers may attempt to collect outstanding co-payments through legal means, such as requesting payment or sending bills, but must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the collected co-payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — October 1, 2025].
similarities: Both sources indicate that providers are generally required to accept Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full for dual-eligible individuals, prohibiting the recovery of certain cost-sharing amounts directly from the member. The policy states this for Medicare Part C benefits [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 1, 2024], while the provider manual specifies it for Medicare Part B benefits for pharmacy providers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025].

Both sources imply that Medicaid acts as a secondary payer to Medicare for dual-eligible members, with policy explicitly stating Medicaid is the payer of last resort [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 1, 2023] and the provider manual detailing that Medicaid only pays for specific drugs not covered by Medicare Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 1, 2025].
differences: The policy guidance specifically addresses co-payment responsibility for Medicare Part C benefits, stating providers cannot recover co-payment or co-insurance amounts directly from dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 1, 2024]. In contrast, the provider manual focuses on pharmacy providers and Medicare Part B benefits, prohibiting them from seeking to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025].

The provider manual provides more detailed instructions regarding the collection of co-payments, stating that providers may attempt to collect outstanding co-payments through legal methods but must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the collected co-payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — October 1, 2025]. Policy documents do not offer this level of detail on the mechanics of co-payment collection for dual-eligible members.

The provider manual explicitly details Medicaid's role in covering prescription drugs for Medicare Part D members, specifying that Medicaid only pays for drug classes legally excluded from Part D plans [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 1, 2025]. Policy documents do not provide this specific detail regarding Part D coverage for dual-eligible members.

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.

Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest relevant policy document (March 1, 2023) and provides specific guidance on Medicaid coverage for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs. The provider manual clarifies that Medicaid will cover certain drug classes specifically excluded by law from Part D, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs, while policy documents generally state that Medicaid will not cover claims if the primary insurer does not cover a medication.
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents generally state that if the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 1, 2020]. For members with Medicare, providers are instructed to bill Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 1, 2020]. The newest policy document, effective March 1, 2023, focuses on adherence to Medicaid requirements in manuals, regulations, and statutes, but does not specifically address Medicare Part D-excluded drugs [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:88 — March 1, 2023].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source, effective October 1, 2025, it is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under Part D plans [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 1, 2025]. Examples of such excluded drugs that Medicaid may cover include select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 1, 2025]. For dual-eligible members, all claims must first be submitted to the Medicare Part D plan [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 1, 2025].
similarities: Both sources imply that Medicaid acts as a payer of last resort or covers items not covered by primary insurance, with the policy document stating, "If the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 1, 2020], and the provider manual specifying conditions under which Medicaid *will* cover drugs for Medicare Part D members (i.e., when they are excluded from Part D coverage) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 1, 2025].
differences: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, explicitly defines specific scenarios where Medicaid *will* cover drugs for Medicare Part D members, namely for drug classes specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage (e.g., select prescription vitamins, certain non-prescription drugs) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 1, 2025]. In contrast, the policy documents, with the newest relevant one effective July 1, 2020, provide a more general statement that "If the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim," without detailing exceptions for Part D-excluded drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 1, 2020].

test_id_17.query_content: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

found within the Appendix A: PA Guidance for DMEPOS section of this issue’s Medicaid Update.

Qualifier- 436-E1 (valid values 01=NDC,09= Healthcare Common Procedure
Coding System (HCPCS).

Procedure Codes and Coverage Guidelines - (alphanumeric form, example
“A4259”) should be submitted in the 11-digit NDC field with leading zeros.

policy.text:
• Provider agencies and practitioners adhere to all Medicaid requirements in
this Manual and in other applicable provider manuals, regulations, and
statutes
• Provider agencies adhere to cultural competency guidelines
• Provider agencies must be knowledgeable and have experience in trauma-
informed care and working with individuals from the cultural groups of those
being served
• The provider agency ensures that staff receive Mandated Reporting training
prior to service delivery
• The provider agency ensures that practitioners maintain the licensure
necessary to provide services under their scope of practice under State law if
applicable
• The provider agency ensures that any insurance required by the designating
State agency is obtained and maintained
• The provider agency ensures that any safety precautions needed to protect
the child population served are taken as necessary and required by the
designating State agency
Additional information and application for Children’s HCBS can be found on the DOH
website.

policy.title: Children�s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children�s Home and Community Based Services Manual
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document (March 1, 2023) and provides specific guidance on Medicaid coverage for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs. The provider manual clarifies that Medicaid will cover certain drug classes specifically excluded by law from Part D, while policy documents generally state that Medicaid will not cover claims if the primary insurer does not cover a medication.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source dated March 1, 2023, provider agencies and practitioners must adhere to all Medicaid requirements in the manual and other applicable provider manuals, regulations, and statutes [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:88 — March 01, 2023]. An older policy document states that if the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 01, 2020].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, it is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under Part D plans [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 01, 2025]. Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources imply that Medicaid's coverage is secondary or conditional to other payers, with the policy document stating Medicaid will not cover claims if the primary insurer does not cover a medication [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 01, 2020], and the provider manual specifying conditions under which Medicaid will pay for Part D-excluded drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly defines specific scenarios where Medicaid will cover drugs for Medicare Part D members, specifically for drug classes excluded by law from Part D coverage, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 01, 2025]. In contrast, the policy documents do not provide this specific detail regarding Part D-excluded drugs, instead offering a general statement that Medicaid will not cover a claim if the primary insurer does not cover the medication [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 01, 2020].

The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more recent and detailed guidance on this specific topic compared to the policy documents, the newest of which is effective March 1, 2023 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 01, 2025] vs. [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:88 — March 01, 2023].

regarding Medicaid fee-for-service covered “Practitioner Administered Drugs” can be found in the

policy.text:
January 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Providers should refer to the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy
Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guide
lines.pdf, for further guidance on origin code and serial number values that must be submitted on the claim
for “pharmacy dispensing” when applicable for non-patient specific orders. Enter a value of “5” in field 419-
DJ (Prescription Origin Code) and a value of “99999999” in field 454- EK (Scheduled Prescription ID
Number). Additionally, providers should refer to the NYS Department of Health (DOH) Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Standard Companion Guide – Transaction Information, located at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
Reminder for Pharmacy and Medical Claims
The National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering provider is required on the NYS Medicaid claim.
Additionally, providers must not bill for the provision of the RSV vaccine to NYS Medicaid members who are
also enrolled in Medicare.

provider_manual.text:
Drugs will be evaluated on a case-by-case basis to determine if they are appropriate to add to the
pharmacy formulary.
Certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and may be
billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that practitioner-administered drugs
must be dispensed as a Pharmacy benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician’s Manual found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a pharmacy benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to the
site of administration. This is considered “white bagging” and is acceptable under the following guidelines:
• Drugs should only be dispensed by the pharmacy directly to the patient when they are to be self-
administered. The policy surrounding self-administered drug delivery is found in section titled Delivery
above.

drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Drugs will be evaluated on a case-by-case basis to determine if they are appropriate to add to the
pharmacy formulary.
Certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and may be
billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that practitioner-administered drugs
must be dispensed as a Pharmacy benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician’s Manual found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a pharmacy benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to the
site of administration. This is considered “white bagging” and is acceptable under the following guidelines:
• Drugs should only be dispensed by the pharmacy directly to the patient when they are to be self-
administered. The policy surrounding self-administered drug delivery is found in section titled Delivery
above.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update from February 1, 2024, and provides specific guidance on Medicaid coverage for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs. The provider manual clarifies that Medicaid will cover certain drug classes specifically excluded by law from Part D, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs, while policy documents generally state that Medicaid will not cover claims if the primary insurer does not cover the medication.
answers.policy_definition: According to the New York State Medicaid Update from July 2020, if the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. This implies that for drugs excluded by Medicare Part D, Medicaid would not cover them if Medicare Part D is considered the primary insurer and does not cover them. The policy documents do not specifically address Medicare Part D-excluded drugs directly, but rather refer to general billing practices when Medicare is involved, stating that providers will bill Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, effective October 1, 2025, explicitly states that Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under Medicare Part D plans [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. Examples of such excluded drugs include select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. It also clarifies that it is generally not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless it falls under these specific exclusions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources acknowledge the role of Medicare as a primary payer for dual-eligible members, with Medicaid acting as a secondary payer or covering specific items not covered by Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly defines specific categories of drugs (e.g., select prescription vitamins, certain non-prescription drugs) that are excluded by law from Medicare Part D coverage and for which Medicaid will pay [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. In contrast, the policy documents do not provide specific examples of Part D-excluded drugs that Medicaid would cover, instead stating a general rule that Medicaid will not cover a claim if the primary insurer does not cover the medication [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01].

The provider manual, being more recent and specific, provides clear guidance on when it is appropriate to bill Medicaid for a Medicare Part D member's prescription drug, specifically for those drug classes legally excluded from Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents offer a more general statement about Medicaid not covering claims if the primary insurer (Medicare Part D in this context) does not cover the medication, without detailing exceptions for legally excluded drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01].

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

also enrolled in Medicare.

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update from May 1, 2024, and should therefore guide current practice. Both sources agree that pharmacies must bill Medicare before Medicaid, as Medicaid is the payor of last resort.
answers.policy_definition: New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:4 — May 01, 2024]. It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, including Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program, and Medicare benefits must be maximized prior to billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]. Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]. Providers are responsible for determining if a patient is covered by other insurance and if that insurance covers the service being billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18 — Jul 23, 2025].
similarities: Both the policy documents and the provider manual state that Medicaid is the payor of last resort and that other applicable third parties, including Medicare, must be billed first [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:4 — May 01, 2024]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources emphasize the pharmacy's responsibility in ensuring proper billing to the correct party before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18 — Jul 23, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that pharmacies enrolled in Medicaid must demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program, a detail not found in the provided policy excerpts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual specifically mentions that services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare, providing a more direct instruction than the general 'bill applicable third parties' found in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Drugs will be evaluated on a case-by-case basis to determine if they are appropriate to add to the
pharmacy formulary.
Certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and may be
billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that practitioner-administered drugs
must be dispensed as a Pharmacy benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician’s Manual found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a pharmacy benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to the
site of administration. This is considered “white bagging” and is acceptable under the following guidelines:
• Drugs should only be dispensed by the pharmacy directly to the patient when they are to be self-
administered. The policy surrounding self-administered drug delivery is found in section titled Delivery
above.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update from May 1, 2024, and should therefore guide current practice. Both sources agree that pharmacies must bill Medicare before Medicaid, as Medicaid is the payor of last resort.
answers.policy_definition: New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties, including Medicare, that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be used first [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:4 — May 01, 2024]. It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, which includes Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]. Medicare benefits must be maximized prior to billing Medicaid, meaning services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]. Providers are responsible for determining if a patient is covered by other insurance and if that insurance covers the billed service [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18 — Jul 23, 2025].
similarities: Both the policy documents and the provider manual state that Medicaid is the payor of last resort and other liable third parties, including Medicare, must be billed first [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:4 — May 01, 2024]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources emphasize the pharmacy's responsibility to ensure proper billing to the correct party before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18 — Jul 23, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that pharmacies enrolled in Medicaid must demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program, a detail not found in the provided policy excerpts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual uses the term "maximized" in relation to Medicare benefits, indicating that all available Medicare benefits should be utilized before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy documents generally refer to billing "applicable third parties" or using "all other available resources" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:4 — May 01, 2024].

test_id_23.query_content: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_23.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies may enroll in Medicaid as a billing provider (also known as a fee-for-service (FFS) provider) or as a
Managed Care Only (non-billing) provider. Fee-for-service pharmacies require Medicare enrollment. Managed
Care Only enrolled pharmacies are not required to enroll in Medicare. NYS Medicaid does not enroll out of state,
mail order, compounding, infusion, Long Term Care (LTC), or specialty pharmacies as FFS billing providers
unless the NYS Department of Health (DOH) determines an unmet need for such pharmacy enrollment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
