provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update from May 1, 2024, and provides more detailed guidance. Both sources agree that pharmacies must bill Medicare before Medicaid, as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
answers.policy_definition: New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:4 — May 01, 2024]. It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, including Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]. Medicare benefits must be maximized prior to billing Medicaid, and services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full, and a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both the policy documents and the provider manual state that Medicaid is the payor of last resort and other available resources, including Medicare, must be billed first [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:4 — May 01, 2024], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources emphasize the pharmacy's responsibility to bill the appropriate party, which includes Medicare, before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual specifies that pharmacies enrolled in Medicaid as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program, a detail not explicitly stated in the provided policy excerpts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual provides specific guidance on reimbursement limits, stating that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee and that Medicare/Medicaid payments must be accepted as payment in full, prohibiting recovery of Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. This level of detail regarding payment caps and acceptance as payment in full is not present in the provided policy documents.

The policy documents mention that Fee-for-service pharmacies require Medicare enrollment, while Managed Care Only enrolled pharmacies are not required to enroll in Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:5 — Feb 01, 2019], a distinction not elaborated upon in the provided provider manual excerpts.

Pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid as a billing provider are required to enroll in Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:5 — 2019-02-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. Medicare benefits must be maximized prior to billing Medicaid, meaning services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full, and a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both the policy documents and the provider manual state that pharmacies must bill Medicare before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:4 — 2024-05-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate that Medicaid is considered the 'payer of last resort' [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:4 — 2024-05-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources require pharmacies enrolled as Medicaid billing providers to also participate in the Medicare Program [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:5 — 2019-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual specifies that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee and that no additional payment will be made if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, a detail not explicitly covered in the provided policy excerpts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that Medicare and Medicaid payments must be accepted as payment in full, and providers cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from Medicaid members, which is not detailed in the policy documents [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.

plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny

• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must

(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.

Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the policy documents, which have a newest effective date of March 1, 2023. Therefore, the provider manual should receive more weight regarding when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare. The provider manual explicitly states that Medicare benefits must be maximized and services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare, while policy documents generally emphasize timely billing to the appropriate party without specific sequencing for Medicare and Medicaid.
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents state that it is the pharmacy's responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, which includes Medicare or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 - July 01, 2020]. Provider agencies and practitioners must adhere to all Medicaid requirements in manuals, regulations, and statutes [Childrens Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Childrens Home and Community Based Services Manual:84 - March 01, 2023].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 - October 01, 2025]. Medicare benefits must be maximized prior to billing Medicaid, meaning services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 - October 01, 2025]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 - October 01, 2025]. Providers cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid recipients [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 - October 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources indicate that pharmacies have a responsibility to bill the appropriate party [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 - July 01, 2020] and adhere to Medicaid requirements [Childrens Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Childrens Home and Community Based Services Manual:84 - March 01, 2023].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that Medicare benefits must be maximized and services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 - October 01, 2025]. In contrast, the policy documents generally state the pharmacy's responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party (Medicare or Medicaid) without specifying the billing order between them [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 - July 01, 2020].

The provider manual details that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only amount and prohibits pharmacies from recovering Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance from Medicaid recipients [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 - October 01, 2025], which is not addressed in the provided policy excerpts.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

ambulatory service.

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 12)
If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior
approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order.
Line (Field 12A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 12 applies. If the prior
approval/authorization number entered in field 12 applies to all claim lines, enter an “A”.
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 13)
If a prior approval/authorization number different from the one entered in field 12 applies to another claim line in the
same claim form, enter the other prior approval/authorization number.

Line (Field 13A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 13 applies.
NOTES:
For information regarding how to obtain Prior Approval/Prior Authorization for specific services, please refer
to the Information for All Providers, Inquiry section on the web page for this manual.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the policy documents, which have a most recent effective date of April 1, 2023. The provider manual indicates that failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility to pursue other coverage before billing Medicaid, while policy documents emphasize resolving prior authorization requirements with the third party prior to submitting to Medicaid.
answers.policy_definition: For crossover claims, prior authorization (PA) requirements must be resolved with the third party prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. Medicaid will not cover a claim if the primary insurer does not cover a medication [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. Providers are required to first bill Medicare or other third-party insurance for services before submitting a claim to Medicaid to ensure Medicaid is the payor of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01]. Some items, such as Long-Acting Reversible Contraceptives (LARC) or other birth control methods, devices, or supplies covered by NYS Medicaid FFS, do not require prior approval [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:6, 7 — 2023-04-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment, and Medicaid regulations require providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. If there is a responsible third party, they must pay first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility to pursue other coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01]. If a prescription/order requires prior approval, the prior approval/authorization number must be entered when billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15, 16 — 2025-07-23].
similarities: Both sources emphasize that Medicaid is the payor of last resort and requires providers to pursue third-party coverage first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources acknowledge the existence and necessity of prior authorization for certain claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15, 16 — 2025-07-23].
differences: The policy documents state that prior authorization requirements must be resolved with the third party prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. In contrast, the provider manual clarifies that failure to obtain prior authorization does not negate the responsibility to pursue other coverage before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Policy documents mention specific items, such as LARC, that do not require prior approval [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:6, 7 — 2023-04-01], while the provider manual does not specify exemptions for prior approval requirements in the provided excerpts.

Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:

Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.

compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 12)
If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior
approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order.
Line (Field 12A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 12 applies. If the prior
approval/authorization number entered in field 12 applies to all claim lines, enter an “A”.
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 13)
If a prior approval/authorization number different from the one entered in field 12 applies to another claim line in the
same claim form, enter the other prior approval/authorization number.

Line (Field 13A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 13 applies.
NOTES:
For information regarding how to obtain Prior Approval/Prior Authorization for specific services, please refer
to the Information for All Providers, Inquiry section on the web page for this manual.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the policy documents, which have a newest effective date of March 1, 2021. Both sources indicate that prior authorization requirements must be addressed with the primary insurer before billing Medicaid, but neither explicitly details requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims.
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents state that providers must first bill Medicare or other third-party insurance for services before submitting a claim to Medicaid to ensure Medicaid is the payor of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — October 1, 2020]. Any claim issues, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, must be resolved with the third party prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 1, 2020]. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will result in denial by Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 1, 2020]. Most items do not require additional authorization prior to dispensing and billing directly to Medicaid, with specific exceptions for Durable Medical Equipment (DME) items [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — March 1, 2021].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Federal regulations and Medicaid regulations (18NYCRR §540.6(e)) require providers to pursue any available other coverage before Medicaid considers payment, meaning the responsible third party must pay first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025]. Failure to obtain prior authorization from the third party does not overcome this responsibility [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025]. The manual also specifies that if a prescription/order requires prior approval or prior authorization, the assigned number should be entered in Field 12 of the billing form [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15, 16 — July 23, 2025]. Prior Authorization (PA) is required for all topical compounded drug products on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:36, 37 — October 1, 2025].
similarities: Both sources emphasize that Medicaid is the payor of last resort and requires providers to bill third-party insurers (like Medicare) first before submitting claims to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — October 1, 2020]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025].

Both sources indicate that prior authorization requirements must be addressed with the primary insurer before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 1, 2020]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that failure to obtain prior authorization from the third party does not overcome the responsibility to bill them first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025], a detail not explicitly covered in the provided policy excerpts.

The policy documents mention that if the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 1, 2020], which is not directly addressed in the provided provider manual excerpts.

The provider manual provides specific instructions for entering prior approval/authorization numbers on billing forms (Field 12) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15, 16 — July 23, 2025], which is not detailed in the policy documents.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

Reasonability Edits for Practitioner-Administered Drugs article published in the August 2015 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2015/
august15_mu.pdf. Additional information can be found in the eMedNY New York State Medicaid Program
Physician – Procedure Codes – Section 2 – Medicine, Drugs, and Drug Administration manual, located at:

with the designated category of service of “0163” (ordered ambulatory diagnostic and treatment
center) or “0282” (hospital-based ordered ambulatory) may bill the invoiced LARC cost as an ordered
ambulatory service.

• Oral cancer drugs,

• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the policy documents, which have a most recent effective date of April 1, 2023. The provider manual indicates that failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility to pursue other coverage before billing Medicaid, while policy documents emphasize resolving prior authorization requirements with the third party prior to submitting to Medicaid.
answers.policy_definition: For crossover claims, prior authorization (PA) requirements must be resolved with the third party prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. Medicaid will not cover a claim if the primary insurer does not cover a medication [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. Providers are required to first bill Medicare or other third-party insurance before submitting a claim to Medicaid to ensure Medicaid is the payor of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01]. Some items, such as Long-Acting Reversible Contraceptives (LARC) or other birth control methods, devices, or supplies covered by NYS Medicaid FFS, do not require prior approval [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:6, 7 — 2023-04-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment, and providers must pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility to pursue other coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01]. If a prescription/order requires prior approval, the provider must enter the assigned prior approval/authorization number when billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15, 16 — 2025-07-23].
similarities: Both sources emphasize that Medicaid is the payor of last resort and requires providers to pursue other available coverage (third-party insurance or Medicare) before submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate that prior authorization may be required for certain services or items, and if so, must be addressed [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15, 16 — 2025-07-23].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility to pursue other coverage" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01], whereas the policy documents state that "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01].

Policy documents mention specific items like LARC that do not require prior approval [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:6, 7 — 2023-04-01], while the provider manual focuses more broadly on the process of entering a prior approval number if one is required [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15, 16 — 2025-07-23].

Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility to bill the third party first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025]. Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — October 1, 2025]. If a prescription/order requires prior approval, the provider must enter the assigned prior approval/authorization number when billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15, 16 — July 23, 2025].
similarities: Both sources emphasize that Medicaid is the payor of last resort and requires providers to bill third-party insurers first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — October 1, 2020] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025].

Both sources indicate that prior authorization requirements with the third party must be addressed before submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 1, 2020] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility" to bill the third party first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025], which is not directly stated in the policy documents.

Policy documents mention specific items like LARC that do not require prior approval for NYS Medicaid FFS [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:6, 7 — April 1, 2023], while the provider manual focuses on the general requirement to enter a prior approval number if one is required [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15, 16 — July 23, 2025].

test_id_30.query_content: For crossover claims, what are the requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment?
test_id_30.concept: crossover claims prior approval after Medicaid payment

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Prior approval (PA) is not required for any LARC, or other birth control method, device, or supply covered by
NYS Medicaid FFS. NYS Medicaid FFS reimbursement for LARC is based on the acquisition cost of the
provider (actual, invoiced cost). For important claims processing rules, providers should refer to the
Reasonability Edits for Practitioner-Administered Drugs article published in the August 2015 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2015/
august15_mu.pdf. Additional information can be found in the eMedNY New York State Medicaid Program
Physician – Procedure Codes – Section 2 – Medicine, Drugs, and Drug Administration manual, located at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/PDFS/Physician_Procedure_Codes_Sect2.pdf.

with the designated category of service of “0163” (ordered ambulatory diagnostic and treatment
center) or “0282” (hospital-based ordered ambulatory) may bill the invoiced LARC cost as an ordered
ambulatory service.

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 12)
If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior
approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order.
Line (Field 12A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 12 applies. If the prior
approval/authorization number entered in field 12 applies to all claim lines, enter an “A”.
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 13)
If a prior approval/authorization number different from the one entered in field 12 applies to another claim line in the
same claim form, enter the other prior approval/authorization number.

Line (Field 13A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 13 applies.
NOTES:
For information regarding how to obtain Prior Approval/Prior Authorization for specific services, please refer
to the Information for All Providers, Inquiry section on the web page for this manual.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the policy documents, which have a most recent effective date of April 1, 2023. The provider manual indicates that failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility to pursue other coverage before billing Medicaid, implying that prior authorization requirements must be met with the primary insurer first. Policy documents also state that claim issues, including prior authorization, must be resolved with the third party before submitting to Medicaid.
answers.policy_definition: Prior authorization (PA) requirements must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. Medicaid will deny claims if the primary insurer does not cover a medication or if the claim is not submitted to the third party first [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. Providers are required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) from third-party payors to Medicaid claims to ensure Medicaid is the payor of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01]. Some items, such as certain Durable Medical Equipment, Prosthetics, Orthotics, Supplies, are not subject to the carve-out and should continue to be billed to the member’s Managed Care (MC) plan [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — 2021-03-01]. Prior approval is not required for certain LARC or other birth control methods, devices, or supplies covered by NYS Medicaid FFS [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:6, 7 — 2023-04-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment, and providers must pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility to bill the primary insurer first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01]. If a prescription/order requires prior approval, the provider must enter the assigned prior approval/authorization number when billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15, 16 — 2025-07-23].
similarities: Both sources emphasize that Medicaid is the payor of last resort and requires providers to pursue other available coverage, including third-party insurance or Medicare, before submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate that prior authorization requirements with the primary insurer must be addressed before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The policy documents explicitly state that providers will be required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) from third-party payors to Medicaid claims [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01], a detail not mentioned in the provided provider manual excerpts.

The policy documents mention specific exemptions for prior approval, such as for LARC or other birth control methods, devices, or supplies covered by NYS Medicaid FFS [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:6, 7 — 2023-04-01], which is not detailed in the provider manual excerpts.

The provider manual explicitly states that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility" to bill the primary insurer first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01], which is a direct statement about the consequence of not obtaining PA with the primary insurer, whereas policy documents state that claim issues, including PA, must be resolved with the third party prior to submitting to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01].

test_id_31.query_content: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.

Update to New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing

NYRx, the New York State (NYS) Medicaid Pharmacy program, needs to ensure accurate Coordination of

there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the patients' health benefits, such as a health
insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient
Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, which has an effective date of April 1, 2025. Both sources consistently state that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring all other available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment, with the provider manual offering more specific guidance on Workers' Compensation cases.
answers.policy_definition: Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first, with NYS Medicaid always acting as the payer of last resort. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be utilized before NYS Medicaid considers payment. If a responsible third-party, such as a health insurance provider, should pay for a patient's health benefits, they must pay first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — April 1, 2025]. NYS Medicaid then pays the lesser of the Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — October 1, 2022].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Coordination of Benefits (COB) requires that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment, as mandated by federal regulations. Providers are required to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025]. If a member has a work-related injury with an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be utilized before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program. However, if a drug claim is not related to a Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025].
similarities: Both sources agree that NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — April 1, 2025] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025].

Both sources state that federal regulations require all other available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — April 1, 2025] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025].
differences: The provider manual provides specific guidance regarding Workers’ Compensation cases, stating that Workers’ Compensation coverage must be used first for work-related injuries, but NYRx can be billed as primary for unrelated conditions with a specific Eligibility Clarification Code [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025]. The policy documents do not detail this specific scenario.

The policy document from October 2022 specifies that NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — October 1, 2022], which is not explicitly mentioned in the provided provider manual excerpts.
