policy.text:
May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Update to New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits Submission
NYRx, the New York State (NYS) Medicaid Pharmacy program, needs to ensure accurate Coordination of
Benefits (COB) submissions are recognized and other patient responsibility amounts are accepted. This is an
update to previous guidance issued in the Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits (COB) Submission article published in the July 2017 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/jul17_
mu.pdf.
Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. If
there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the patients' health benefits, such as a health
insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient
Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text: If all three conditions are not met, the billing provider may call the Department for assistance.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
To qualify for Medicaid payment, a compounded prescription must include:
• A combination of any two (2) or more legend drugs found on the list of Medicaid Reimbursable Prescription
Drugs, or
• A combination of any legend drug(s) included on the list of Medicaid Reimbursable Prescription Drugs and any
other item(s) not commercially available as an ethical or proprietary product(s) or
• A combination of two (2) or more products which are labeled “Caution: For Manufacturing Purposes Only”.
For example:
• The combination of Aquaphor and Hydrocortisone Cream 2.5% is NOT considered a compound since it does
not meet any of the above requirements.
• Intravenous prescription products that require reconstitution, further measurement, dilution and/or instillation
into a suitable device (i.e., mini-bag, IV reservoir or syringe) for administration are not considered to have been
compounded and should not be submitted for reimbursement as compounds.
NYS Medicaid compound policy:
• Only the dispensing pharmacy may prepare the prescribed compounded prescription.
• Dispensing outsourced prepared compounds is not allowable.
• Compounds trademarked by pharmacies are not coverable.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, which has an effective date of April 1, 2025. Both sources consistently state that NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring providers to bill all other liable third parties before submitting claims to Medicaid.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source, New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be utilized before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 1, 2025]. This requirement ensures that the correct party pays first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6, 7 — October 1, 2022].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment, and Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) mandate that a provider pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025]. If a responsible third party should pay for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as the primary payor, that third party must pay first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025]. For work-related injuries with an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be utilized before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program. However, if a drug claim is not related to a Workers’ Compensation case, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025].
similarities: Both the policy documents and the provider manual state that NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 1, 2025; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025].

Both sources emphasize that federal regulations require all other available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 1, 2025; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025].

Both require providers to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 1, 2025; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly details specific scenarios for Coordination of Benefits, such as those involving Workers’ Compensation, and provides instructions for billing NYRx as primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” if the claim is unrelated to a Workers’ Compensation case [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025]. The policy documents do not provide this level of specific scenario guidance within the provided excerpts.

test_id_33.query_content: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Additional information regarding Coordination
of Benefits (COB) can be found in the following Medicaid Update articles:
• Update on Coordination of Benefits Submissions for Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing article
published in the October 2022 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at: https://
www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2022/docs/mu_no12_oct22_pr.pdf;
• Reminder: Medicaid Requires Coordination of Benefits article published in the April 2022 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2022/
docs/mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf;
• Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients with Other Coverage article published in the
November 2021 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/
health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/docs/mu_no13_nov21_pr.pdf; and
• Attention Pharmacy Providers: Coordination of Benefits (COB) Processing Change for Other Payer
ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier article published in the October 2017 issue of the Medicaid Update,
located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/oct17_mu.pdf.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.

o Medicaid members younger than 21 years old.
o Medicaid members during pregnancy and for the two months after the month in which the
pregnancy ends.
o Residents of Adult Care Facilities licensed by the New York State Department of Health (DOH).
o Residents of nursing homes.
o Residents of Intermediate Care Facilities for the Developmentally Disabled (ICF/DD).

o Members in Comprehensive Medicaid Case Management (CMCM) or Service Coordination

o Members in a DOH HCBS Waiver Program for Persons with Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI).

o Members in Hospice.
o American Indians and Alaska Natives who have ever received a service from the Indian Health
Service, tribal health programs or under contract health services referral.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document dated April 1, 2025, and should therefore receive more weight. Both sources agree that New York State Medicaid is the payer of last resort and requires providers to bill other liable third parties first.
answers.policy_definition: New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first, meaning if there is a responsible third-party health insurance provider, they should pay first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6, 7 — 2022-10-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment, and Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. If a responsible third party should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as the primary payor, that third party must pay first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. For work-related injuries with an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. However, if a drug claim is not related to a Workers’ Compensation case, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources state that New York State Medicaid is always the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources require providers to bill applicable third parties or utilize other available resources before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual specifically mentions Workers’ Compensation as a third-party resource that must be utilized prior to billing NYRx Medicaid for work-related injuries, while also providing an exception for non-work-related claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents do not detail specific types of third-party liability beyond general health insurance providers.

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Fee-for-Service Dental Claim Submission Guidance
When Third-Party Liability Payment Involved
New York State (NYS) Medicaid continues to work to increase provider compliance with properly reporting
correct Coordination of Benefits (COB) information on claims submitted for beneficiaries who have primary
insurance. The COB claim to NYS Medicaid must accurately reflect the primary payers’ adjudication of the
claim as reported in the Explanation of Benefits (EOB).
When the primary insurance is a Medicare Advantage Plan / Medicare Part C with dental coverage:
• Claims for services involving a third-party payment by a Medicare Advantage Plan greater than zero
dollars (>$0) must be submitted using the electronic claim format. This also includes electronic
submission of by-report procedure codes if a payment was made by a Medicare Pact C Plan.
• The payor code for Medicare Advantage Plans is the 16-health maintenance organization (HMO) and
must be indicated on all electronic claims when Medicare Advantage is primary.

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides the most current and detailed requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB), emphasizing that Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. While the newest policy update from April 1, 2025, reiterates this principle, the provider manual offers more specific scenarios and exceptions, and should receive more weight for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01]. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be utilized before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01]. Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — 2022-10-01]. Providers must accurately reflect the primary payers’ adjudication of the claim as reported in the Explanation of Benefits (EOB) when submitting COB claims to NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment, and Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) mandate providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. If there is a responsible third party, they must pay first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not negate the responsibility to coordinate benefits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. For work-related injuries with an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be utilized before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. However, if a drug claim is not related to a Workers’ Compensation case, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources agree that NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort and that federal regulations require all other available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources state that providers are required to bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual provides specific scenarios for COB, such as requirements for Workers’ Compensation cases and exceptions for unrelated drug claims, which are not detailed in the policy documents [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility to coordinate benefits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01], a point not directly addressed in the provided policy excerpts.

Policy documents mention the requirement for COB claims to accurately reflect the primary payers’ adjudication as reported in the Explanation of Benefits (EOB) [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01], which is not explicitly detailed in the provided provider manual excerpts.

Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Additional information regarding Coordination
of Benefits (COB) can be found in the following Medicaid Update articles:
• Update on Coordination of Benefits Submissions for Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing article
published in the October 2022 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at: https://
www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2022/docs/mu_no12_oct22_pr.pdf;
• Reminder: Medicaid Requires Coordination of Benefits article published in the April 2022 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2022/
docs/mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf;
• Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients with Other Coverage article published in the
November 2021 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/
health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/docs/mu_no13_nov21_pr.pdf; and
• Attention Pharmacy Providers: Coordination of Benefits (COB) Processing Change for Other Payer
ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier article published in the October 2017 issue of the Medicaid Update,
located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/oct17_mu.pdf.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Update to New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits Submission
NYRx, the New York State (NYS) Medicaid Pharmacy program, needs to ensure accurate Coordination of
Benefits (COB) submissions are recognized and other patient responsibility amounts are accepted. This is an
update to previous guidance issued in the Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits (COB) Submission article published in the July 2017 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/jul17_
mu.pdf.
Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. If
there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the patients' health benefits, such as a health
insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient
Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Exhibit 2.4.1.2-1 shows the claim as it was originally submitted and
Exhibit 2.4.1.2-2 shows the claim being submitted as voided.
Exhibit 2.4.1.2-1

Exhibit 2.4.1.2-2
Recipient ID Number (Field 5)
Enter the member ID. This information may be obtained from the member’s Common Benefit ID Card (CBIC).
Date of Birth (Field 6)
Enter the member’s birth date. This information may be obtained from the CBIC. The birth date must be in the format
MMDDYYYY as shown in Exhibit 2.4.1-3.

Exhibit 2.4.1-3
Sex (Field 7)
Place an ‘X’ in the appropriate box to indicate the member’s sex. This information may be obtained from the CBIC.
Recipient Other Insurance Code (Field 8)
If the member is exempt from co-pay, enter the value Z9 in this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, which has an effective date of April 1, 2025. Both sources consistently state that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring all other available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment, with the provider manual offering more specific guidance on Workers' Compensation cases.
answers.policy_definition: Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first, with NYS Medicaid always acting as the payer of last resort. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources must be utilized before NYS Medicaid considers payment. If a responsible third-party, such as a health insurance provider, should pay for a patient's health benefits, they must pay first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — April 1, 2025]. NYS Medicaid then pays the lesser of the Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — October 1, 2022]. Providers are required to bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — April 1, 2025].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Coordination of Benefits (COB) requires that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment, as mandated by federal regulations. Providers are required by Medicaid regulations (18NYCRR §540.6(e)) to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025]. If a responsible third party is the primary payer for a Medicaid member's health benefits, they must pay first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025]. For Workers’ Compensation cases, if a member has an open case for a work-related injury, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be used before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program. However, if a drug claim is not related to a Workers’ Compensation case (e.g., maintenance medication for an unrelated chronic condition), NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025].
similarities: Both sources agree that NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — April 1, 2025] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025].

Both sources state that federal regulations require all other available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — April 1, 2025] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025].

Both sources emphasize that if a responsible third-party should pay, they must pay first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — April 1, 2025] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025].
differences: The provider manual provides specific guidance regarding Workers’ Compensation cases, detailing when Workers’ Compensation coverage must be utilized and when NYRx may be billed as the primary payer for unrelated conditions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025], which is not explicitly detailed in the provided policy documents.

The policy documents mention that NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — October 1, 2022], a detail not present in the provided provider manual excerpts.

test_id_36.query_content: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_36.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
A supervising pharmacist must adhere to the following
responsibilities:
• The supervising pharmacist is responsible for the proper conduct of the pharmacy.
• The supervising pharmacist oversees the practice of other employed pharmacists and ensures that
those unlicensed persons do not engage in unlawful activity.
• The supervising pharmacist must have an active role in the conduct of the pharmacy and not a passive
role.
• The supervising pharmacist may not assign their responsibility to others.
• The supervising pharmacist must be aware of all laws and regulations and must instruct and direct
others to ensure compliance.
• The supervising pharmacist must work full-time (at least 30 hours per week). If the establishment is
operating less than 30 hours per week, the supervising pharmacist must work the majority of the hours.
• If the supervising pharmacist is to be absent from a pharmacy at any time, the supervising pharmacist
must ensure another qualified pharmacist is present in their absence.
• The supervising pharmacist cannot supervise more than one pharmacy establishment at a time.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies are not required to generate and keep a hard copy of electronic prescriptions and fiscal orders if they are
securely stored and maintained. When stored electronically, the electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal orders
that were e-prescribed must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be
authenticated.
Telephone Orders
Prescribers may telephone prescriptions and fiscal orders for drugs directly to a pharmacy unless otherwise prohibited
by state or federal law or regulations. The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the
prescriber's identity and validity of the prescription if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist.
• Telephoned non-controlled prescription drug orders are considered original.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual (effective 2025-10-01) is more recent than the New York State Medicaid Update policy documents (newest effective 2023-07-01) and provides more specific guidance on pharmacists' responsibilities when patient or prescriber information is missing. The provider manual indicates pharmacists are not required to enter missing patient information if it's readily available or the prescription is deemed legitimate, and they must make a good faith effort to verify unknown prescribers. Policy documents primarily address notifying prescribers for prior authorization of non-preferred drugs.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source, the New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 (effective 2023-07-01), when presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires prior authorization (PA), the pharmacist must notify the prescriber. PA requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or an authorized agent of the prescriber [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01]. Older policy documents state that pharmacy owners and supervising pharmacists have a responsibility to ensure recognized standards of pharmacy practice are maintained [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:12 — 2023-04-01] and that the supervising pharmacist is responsible for the proper conduct of the pharmacy [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:9 — 2023-05-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source, the New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines (effective 2025-10-01), if the address, age, or CIN of a Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter these items on the prescription if the information is readily available in the pharmacy's records and the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]. Additionally, the pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources imply a general responsibility for pharmacists to ensure proper prescription processing and adherence to standards, though they address different aspects of missing information. The policy documents state pharmacists must ensure recognized standards of pharmacy practice are maintained [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:12 — 2023-04-01], while the provider manual outlines specific actions for missing patient data or unknown prescribers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5, 27, 28 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual directly addresses missing patient information (address, age, CIN) and missing prescriber identity, stating pharmacists are not required to enter missing patient data if it's readily available or the prescription is legitimate, and they must verify unknown prescribers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5, 27, 28 — 2025-10-01]. In contrast, the policy documents, specifically the newest one, focus on the pharmacist's responsibility to notify the prescriber when a non-preferred drug requires prior authorization, rather than addressing missing patient or prescriber identification details on the prescription itself [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01].

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document (July 1, 2023) and provides specific guidance on pharmacists' responsibilities when patient information is missing, stating they are not required to enter it if readily available or if the prescription is legitimate. Policy documents primarily address pharmacist responsibilities for prior authorization and general pharmacy conduct, but do not directly address missing patient or prescriber information.
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents indicate that when presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug requiring prior authorization (PA), the pharmacist must notify the prescriber [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01]. General responsibilities for pharmacy owners and supervising pharmacists include ensuring recognized standards of pharmacy practice are maintained and that the pharmacy is under the immediate supervision of a licensed pharmacist [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:12 — 2023-04-01]. A supervising pharmacist is responsible for the proper conduct of the pharmacy and oversees other employed pharmacists [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:9 — 2023-05-01]. However, policy documents do not specifically detail pharmacists' responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing from a prescription itself, beyond the PA notification requirement.
answers.provider_manual_definition: If the address, age, or CIN of a Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter these items on the prescription if the information is readily available in the pharmacy's records and the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]. The pharmacist is also responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources imply a general responsibility for pharmacists to ensure the legitimacy and proper handling of prescriptions. The provider manual states pharmacists must make a "good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01], while policy documents state pharmacists must ensure "recognized standards of pharmacy practice are maintained" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:12 — 2023-04-01].
differences: The provider manual directly addresses the scenario of missing patient information (address, age, CIN), stating pharmacists are not required to enter it if readily available or if the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents do not provide specific guidance on missing patient or prescriber information on the prescription itself, but rather focus on notifying the prescriber for prior authorization of non-preferred drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01].

The provider manual explicitly states the pharmacist's responsibility to verify the prescriber's identity if unknown [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01], a detail not found in the provided policy excerpts.

test_id_38.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
A supervising pharmacist must adhere to the following
responsibilities:
• The supervising pharmacist is responsible for the proper conduct of the pharmacy.
• The supervising pharmacist oversees the practice of other employed pharmacists and ensures that
those unlicensed persons do not engage in unlawful activity.
• The supervising pharmacist must have an active role in the conduct of the pharmacy and not a passive
role.
• The supervising pharmacist may not assign their responsibility to others.
• The supervising pharmacist must be aware of all laws and regulations and must instruct and direct
others to ensure compliance.
• The supervising pharmacist must work full-time (at least 30 hours per week). If the establishment is
operating less than 30 hours per week, the supervising pharmacist must work the majority of the hours.
• If the supervising pharmacist is to be absent from a pharmacy at any time, the supervising pharmacist
must ensure another qualified pharmacist is present in their absence.
• The supervising pharmacist cannot supervise more than one pharmacy establishment at a time.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies are not required to generate and keep a hard copy of electronic prescriptions and fiscal orders if they are
securely stored and maintained. When stored electronically, the electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal orders
that were e-prescribed must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be
authenticated.
Telephone Orders
Prescribers may telephone prescriptions and fiscal orders for drugs directly to a pharmacy unless otherwise prohibited
by state or federal law or regulations. The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the
prescriber's identity and validity of the prescription if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist.
• Telephoned non-controlled prescription drug orders are considered original.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document (July 1, 2023) and provides specific guidance on pharmacists' responsibilities when patient information is missing, stating they are not required to enter it if readily available or if the prescription is legitimate. Policy documents, while not directly addressing missing patient/prescriber information, outline general pharmacist responsibilities for proper conduct and ensuring standards of practice.
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents do not directly address the responsibilities of pharmacists when required patient or prescriber information is missing. However, they state that pharmacy owners and supervising pharmacists have a responsibility to ensure recognized standards of pharmacy practice are maintained and that the pharmacy is under the immediate supervision of a licensed pharmacist [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:12 — April 1, 2023]. Supervising pharmacists are also responsible for the proper conduct of the pharmacy and overseeing other employed pharmacists [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:9 — May 1, 2023]. For non-preferred drugs requiring prior authorization, the pharmacist must notify the prescriber when presented with such a prescription [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — July 1, 2023].
answers.provider_manual_definition: When the address, age, or CIN of a Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter these items on the prescription if the information is readily available in the pharmacy's records and the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — October 1, 2025]. The pharmacist is also responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — October 1, 2025].
similarities: Both sources imply a general responsibility for pharmacists to ensure the legitimacy and proper handling of prescriptions, with the provider manual explicitly stating the pharmacist's responsibility to verify prescription validity and prescriber identity if unknown [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — October 1, 2025] and policy documents stating pharmacists must maintain recognized standards of pharmacy practice [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:12 — April 1, 2023].
differences: The provider manual directly addresses the specific scenario of missing patient information (address, age, CIN), stating pharmacists are not required to enter it if readily available or if the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — October 1, 2025]. Policy documents do not provide specific guidance for this scenario.

The provider manual explicitly outlines the pharmacist's responsibility to make a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if unknown [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — October 1, 2025]. Policy documents do not detail this specific responsibility but mention the pharmacist's role in notifying the prescriber for prior authorization of non-preferred drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — July 1, 2023].

similarities: Both sources imply a general responsibility for pharmacists to ensure the legitimacy and proper handling of prescriptions, with the provider manual explicitly stating the pharmacist's responsibility to verify the validity of the prescription and prescriber's identity if unknown [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — October 01, 2025] and policy documents stating supervising pharmacists must ensure recognized standards of pharmacy practice are maintained [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:12 — April 01, 2023].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses missing patient information (address, age, CIN), stating pharmacists are not required to enter it if readily available or if they are satisfied the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — October 01, 2025]. Policy documents do not provide specific guidance on missing patient information but instead focus on the pharmacist's responsibility to notify the prescriber for prior authorization of non-preferred drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — July 01, 2023].

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).

April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.

• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.

additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, and the newest policy source dated January 1, 2025, weight loss drugs are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid. The provider manual is more recent and should receive more weight by default for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used for weight loss. Specifically, indications for weight loss are not covered pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 - 2025-01-01]. This exclusion also applies to agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain, as per New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3) [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 - 2022-04-01]. Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 - 2024-04-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are examples of drugs/drug uses that are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 - 2025-10-01]. Additionally, compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 - 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both the policy documents and the provider manual explicitly state that drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 - 2025-01-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 - 2025-10-01].

Both sources cite the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as a basis for the exclusion of drugs for weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 - 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 - 2025-10-01].

Both sources mention that compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, including weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 - 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 - 2025-10-01].
differences: The policy documents specifically reference New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3) as an additional basis for excluding agents used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 - 2022-04-01], which is not explicitly mentioned in the provided provider manual excerpts.

The policy documents provide an example of a specific drug, Wegovy, and explicitly state its exclusion for weight loss indications [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 - 2025-01-01], whereas the provider manual excerpts do not list specific drug examples for this exclusion.

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, indicates that drugs for weight loss are an excluded benefit. This aligns with policy documents, with the newest policy update from January 1, 2025, also stating that drugs for weight loss are not reimbursable. The provider manual is more recent and should receive more weight.
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain, as per New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3) [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 - April 01, 2022]. Additionally, compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss, as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 - April 01, 2024]. The New York State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs generally excludes drugs not listed, and examples of non-reimbursable drugs include those for weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 - January 01, 2025].
answers.provider_manual_definition: NYS Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, and drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are explicitly listed as examples of drugs/drug uses not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 - October 01, 2025]. Furthermore, compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), which includes drugs to treat weight loss [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 - October 01, 2025].
similarities: Both policy and provider manual documents explicitly state that drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 - April 01, 2022; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 - October 01, 2025].

Both sources refer to the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as a basis for excluding drugs for weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 - April 01, 2024; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 - October 01, 2025].

Both sources indicate that compounds containing excluded drugs or made for excluded indications, such as weight loss, are not covered [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 - April 01, 2024; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 - October 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, is the most recent document and provides the current guidance on this exclusion [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 - October 01, 2025], while the newest policy update addressing this specific exclusion is from January 1, 2025 [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 - January 01, 2025].
