• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmaceutical Management
Overview
Drug Utilization Review (DUR) programs are intended to assure that prescriptions for outpatient drugs are appropriate,
medically necessary, and not likely to result in adverse medical consequences. DUR programs help to ensure that the
patient receives the proper medicine at the right time in the correct dose and dosage form.
The benefits of DUR programs may include reduced Medicaid costs, reduced hospital admissions, improved health for
Medicaid members, increased coordination of health care services, and reduced drug diversion. Information supplied
to Medicaid pharmacy providers through the DUR programs may enhance their ability to prescribe and dispense
medication more appropriately.
The federal legislation requiring states to implement DUR programs also requires states to establish DUR Boards whose
function is to play a major role in each state's DUR program. The Department of Health contains a DUR Board
comprised of health care professionals with recognized knowledge and expertise appointed by the Commissioner.
More information regarding the DUR Board and Program may be found here:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/dur/.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Weight loss drugs are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid according to both policy and provider manual documents. The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, which has an effective date of January 1, 2025, and should therefore receive more weight.
answers.policy_definition: Indications for weight loss are not covered pursuant to Social Security Act (SSA) §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 - 2025-01-01]. This exclusion applies to agents used for the treatment of weight loss for both Fee-for-Service (FFS) and Managed Care (MMC) program areas [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 - 2022-04-01]. Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss, as per SSA §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 - 2024-04-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the Social Security Act (SSA) §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 - 2025-10-01]. While the provided excerpts do not explicitly list "weight loss" as an excluded indication in the provider manual, the reference to SSA §1927(d)(2) aligns with the policy documents that specify weight loss drugs as excluded under this section.
similarities: Both policy and provider manual documents indicate that drugs for excluded indications, as per Social Security Act (SSA) §1927(d)(2), are not covered [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 - 2025-01-01], [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 - 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 - 2025-10-01].

Both sources state that compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 - 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 - 2025-10-01].
differences: The policy documents explicitly state that "agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain" are exclusions [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 - 2022-04-01] and that "Indications for weight loss are not covered pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2)" [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 - 2025-01-01]. The provider manual, while referencing SSA §1927(d)(2) for excluded indications, does not explicitly list "weight loss" in the provided excerpts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 - 2025-10-01].

Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

provider_manual.text:
NYRx covers certain
unapproved drugs as permitted by federal law, in circumstances such as a drug shortage of an approved drug, or when
the Program has determined the drug to be medically necessary, such that without the drug it would cause a
significant impact in the health of a Medicaid member, and when there is no FDA approved alternative to treat the
medical condition. NYRx has ensured that the formulary coverage of non-approved drugs is limited to those that are
identified by the FDA as on shortage, or those that are considered to be medically necessary when there is not an FDA
approved suitable alternative.

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, indicates that drugs for weight loss are an excluded benefit, aligning with policy updates from January 2025 and April 2024. The provider manual is more recent and should receive more weight for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: Weight loss drugs are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid. The January 2025 Medicaid Update explicitly states that "Indications for weight loss are not covered pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2)" [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — January 1, 2025]. Similarly, the April 2024 Medicaid Update notes that compounds "may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2)... including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — April 1, 2024]. An earlier April 2022 update also reminded providers that "agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain" are exclusions for both FFS and MMC program areas [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — April 1, 2022].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Drugs used for the treatment of weight loss are not reimbursable by Medicaid. The New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual, effective October 1, 2025, states that "The following are examples of drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation: • Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 1, 2025]. It also specifies that compounds "may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2)" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — October 1, 2025].
similarities: Both the policy documents and the provider manual explicitly state that drugs for weight loss are an excluded benefit [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — January 1, 2025], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 1, 2025].

Both sources reference the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as the basis for excluding certain indications, including weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — January 1, 2025], [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — April 1, 2024], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — October 1, 2025].

Both sources indicate that compounds may not be made for excluded indications such as weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — April 1, 2024], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — October 1, 2025].
differences: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update specifically addressing weight loss drugs, which is effective January 1, 2025 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 1, 2025], [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — January 1, 2025].

The policy documents provide a specific example of a weight loss drug, Wegovy, and explicitly state its exclusion based on SSA §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — January 1, 2025], whereas the provider manual provides a general statement about drugs used for weight loss without naming specific examples.

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the

dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.

code for the dosage form.

contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).

The New York State Medicaid Update from April 2022 explicitly states that "agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain" are exclusions for both FFS and MMC program areas, referencing NYCRR Title 18 §505.3(g)(3) [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — April 01, 2022]. policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The information in this article is intended to advise
providers, who may be new to submitting claims to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, that
all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be
submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost.
Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care
(MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim. It is the responsibility of the
covered entity (CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with
the 340B stock of the CE for 340B-eligible NYS Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for
these drugs. If rebates are received by the NYS DOH for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Ingredient cost submitted exceeds 340B ceiling price
2. Invalid codes submitted for 340B pharmacy drugs
The 340B ceiling price refers to the maximum amount that a manufacturer can charge the covered entity for
the purchase of a 340B drug. A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid that is higher than the 340B ceiling price
will be denied. Claims that are denied must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost. Alternatively, the
pharmacy may dispense a non-340B drug and remove the claim level identifiers and submit at the usual and
customary charge.
Claims for 340B drugs submitted to NYS Medicaid via the NCPDP D.0 format must also adhere to the
following:
• must be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) members and Medicaid Managed
Care (MMC) enrollees; and
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members and MMC enrollees,
net any manufacturer discounts and/or other price reductions.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
It is the responsibility of the covered entity
(CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with the 340B stock
of the CE for 340B-eligible Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for these drugs. If rebates
are received by the NYS Department of Health (DOH) for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims. Please note: 340B claims billed to Medicaid FFS as secondary claims also
require identifiers, as well as 340B claims billed to MMC Plans.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pricing methodology is systematically determined as follows:
Drug Type If NADAC is available If NADAC is unavailable, Professional Dispensing Fee*
reimburse at: reimburse at:
Lower of NADAC, FUL, Lower of WAC – 17.5%,
Generics SMAC or U&C FUL, SMAC, or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC or
Brands U&C Lower of WAC or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC, FUL,
OTCs SMAC or U&C Lower of WAC, FUL, $10.18
SMAC, or U&C
340B**Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost 340B Acquisition Cost $10.18
Drug
Note: Claims will pay at the pharmacy's Usual and Customary (U&C) pricing if lower than drug ingredient cost plus
dispensing fee.
* Professional Dispensing Fee applies if the drug meets the definition of Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at
U&C.
**When claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
below for more information.
Federal Upper Limit (FUL) is determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a price ceiling used by Centers
for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is
considered fraudulent billing.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in the NCPDP format:
Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field Medicaid Primary Claim (Medicare. Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 N/A
409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost N/A
Lowest Net Charge to Cash Lowest Net Charge to Cash
426-DQ Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) Customers Customers
FAQs on the 340B program itself, as well as information on how to ask additional questions, can be found on the HRSA
website at: https://www.hrsa.gov/opa/faqs/index.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, which is effective February 1, 2024. Therefore, the provider manual should receive more weight for current guidance on 340B drug reimbursement. Both sources indicate that 340B drugs must be identified on claims and are reimbursed based on acquisition cost, with claims exceeding the 340B ceiling price being denied.
answers.policy_definition: 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost, and claims submitted to NYS Medicaid that are higher than the 340B ceiling price will be denied [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — February 1, 2024]. Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all billed drugs and must identify 340B drugs by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — July 1, 2022]. For claims submitted to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, 340B-purchased drugs must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — June 1, 2023]. Incorrect or missing claim level identifiers can lead to the covered entity being responsible for reimbursing manufacturers for 340B discounts if rebates are collected by the NYS Department of Health [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — November 1, 2021].
answers.provider_manual_definition: 340B purchased drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost, plus a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — October 1, 2025]. Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts, meaning manufacturers are not required to provide both a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — October 1, 2025]. 340B covered entities must determine if they will use 340B drugs for Medicaid patients [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — October 1, 2025]. Claims for 340B drugs submitted in NCPDP format require specific fields, including Submission Clarification Code (SCC) '20', Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) '08' for Medicaid Primary Claims, and Ingredient Cost Submitted as '340B Acquisition Cost' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — October 1, 2025]. Applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is considered fraudulent billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — October 1, 2025].
similarities: Both sources indicate that 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — June 1, 2023] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — October 1, 2025].

Both sources emphasize the importance of correctly identifying 340B drugs on claims to avoid issues with rebates and ensure proper reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — November 1, 2021] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — October 1, 2025].

Both sources imply that claims for 340B drugs must adhere to specific pricing limits, with the policy explicitly stating claims higher than the 340B ceiling price will be denied [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — February 1, 2024].
differences: The policy documents mention the use of a 'UD modifier' for 340B drug lines on FFS-only claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — July 1, 2022], while the provider manual specifies NCPDP format requirements including 'Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20' and 'Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08' for Medicaid 340B drug claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — October 1, 2025].

The provider manual explicitly states a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 for 340B purchased drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — October 1, 2025], which is not detailed in the provided policy excerpts.

The provider manual explicitly states that applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is considered fraudulent billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — October 1, 2025], a specific warning not found in the policy excerpts, although the policy does mention consequences for incorrect identifiers.

pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document (February 1, 2024) and should therefore receive more weight regarding 340B drug reimbursement. Both sources indicate that 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost and require specific identifiers for claims processing.
answers.policy_definition: For 340B drugs, claims submitted to NYS Medicaid that exceed the 340B ceiling price will be denied and must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — February 1, 2024]. All 340B-purchased drug claims, when NYS Medicaid is the primary payor, must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — June 1, 2023]. For Fee-For-Service (FFS) only claims, the UD modifier must be appended to the 340B drug line [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — July 1, 2022]. Covered entities are responsible for correctly identifying claims dispensed with 340B stock for eligible Medicaid patients to prevent duplicate rebates [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — November 1, 2021].
answers.provider_manual_definition: 340B purchased drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost, along with a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — October 1, 2025]. Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts, meaning manufacturers are not required to provide both a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — October 1, 2025]. Specific claim level identifiers are required for Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in NCPDP format, including Submission Clarification Code (SCC) '20', Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) '08' for primary claims, and the 340B Acquisition Cost for Ingredient Cost Submitted [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — October 1, 2025]. Applying these identifiers to non-340B purchased drug claims is considered fraudulent billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — October 1, 2025].
similarities: Both sources indicate that 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — June 1, 2023] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — October 1, 2025].

Both sources require specific claim level identifiers for 340B drug claims to ensure proper processing and prevent duplicate discounts [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — June 1, 2023] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — October 1, 2025].
differences: The policy documents mention the use of a 'UD modifier' for 340B drug lines on FFS-only claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — July 1, 2022], while the provider manual specifies NCPDP format fields like 'Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20' and 'Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08' for Medicaid 340B drug claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — October 1, 2025].

The provider manual explicitly states a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 for 340B purchased drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — October 1, 2025], which is not detailed in the provided policy excerpts.

Policy documents state that claims exceeding the 340B ceiling price will be denied and require resubmission with the correct ingredient cost [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — February 1, 2024], a specific detail not found in the provided provider manual excerpts.

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The information in this article is intended to advise
providers, who may be new to submitting claims to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, that
all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be
submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost.
Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care
(MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim. It is the responsibility of the
covered entity (CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with
the 340B stock of the CE for 340B-eligible NYS Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for
these drugs. If rebates are received by the NYS DOH for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Ingredient cost submitted exceeds 340B ceiling price
2. Invalid codes submitted for 340B pharmacy drugs
The 340B ceiling price refers to the maximum amount that a manufacturer can charge the covered entity for
the purchase of a 340B drug. A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid that is higher than the 340B ceiling price
will be denied. Claims that are denied must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost. Alternatively, the
pharmacy may dispense a non-340B drug and remove the claim level identifiers and submit at the usual and
customary charge.
Claims for 340B drugs submitted to NYS Medicaid via the NCPDP D.0 format must also adhere to the
following:
• must be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) members and Medicaid Managed
Care (MMC) enrollees; and
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members and MMC enrollees,
net any manufacturer discounts and/or other price reductions.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Outpatient Dispensed Drugs (NCPDP)
As previously communicated in the August 2019 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled New System
Edit to be Implemented to Validate the Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs, available at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2019/aug19_mu.pdf, for a Medicaid FFS
primary claim only, system editing will compare the ingredient cost submitted (NCPDP field 409-DK) with the
340B ceiling price for the product, as defined by Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA), to
ensure the ingredient cost submitted is the 340B price. The 340B ceiling price refers to the maximum amount
that a manufacturer can charge the CE for the purchase of a 340B covered outpatient drug. A claim submitted
to Medicaid should never be higher than the 340B ceiling price. Any pharmacy that submits a 340B drug
claim, whenever the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling price, will be returned the Medicaid
Eligibility Verification System (MEVS) Rx Denial code “708: Exceeds NY Allowed Maximum” and the
NCPDP Reject code "23: M/I Ingredient Cost”.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

SMAC, or U&C

dispensing fee.
* Professional Dispensing Fee applies if the drug meets the definition of Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at
U&C.
**When claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
below for more information.
Federal Upper Limit (FUL) is determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a price ceiling used by Centers
for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update from February 1, 2024, and should therefore receive more weight regarding 340B drug reimbursement. Both sources indicate that 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost and are subject to a 340B ceiling price, with specific claim identifiers required.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source dated February 1, 2024, claims for 340B pharmacy drugs will be denied if the ingredient cost submitted exceeds the 340B ceiling price, which is defined as the maximum amount a manufacturer can charge the covered entity for the drug. Denied claims must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost, or a non-340B drug may be dispensed with the removal of claim level identifiers [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 - 2024-02-01]. Previously, in June 2023, it was stated that all 340B-purchased drug claims, when NYS Medicaid is the primary payor, must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 - 2023-06-01]. For FFS-only claims, the UD modifier must be appended to the 340B drug line [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 - 2022-07-01]. System editing compares the submitted ingredient cost with the 340B ceiling price for Medicaid FFS primary claims [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 - 2021-11-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, 340B purchased drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost, along with a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 - 2025-10-01]. Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts, meaning manufacturers are not required to provide both a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 - 2025-10-01]. Specific fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in NCPDP format, including Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 for Medicaid Primary Claims, and the Ingredient Cost Submitted as the 340B Acquisition Cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 - 2025-10-01]. Applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is considered fraudulent billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 - 2025-10-01]. The 340B ceiling price is defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate percentage (Unit Rebate Amount or URA) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 - 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources indicate that 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 - 2023-06-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 - 2025-10-01].

Both sources mention the 340B ceiling price as a factor in reimbursement, with claims exceeding this price being denied or requiring correction [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 - 2024-02-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 - 2025-10-01].

Both sources require specific claim level identifiers for 340B drug claims [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 - 2023-06-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 - 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states the professional dispensing fee of $10.18 for 340B purchased drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 - 2025-10-01], which is not detailed in the policy documents.

The policy documents specify the use of the UD modifier for 340B drug lines on FFS-only claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 - 2022-07-01], while the provider manual details specific NCPDP fields (e.g., 420-DK, 423-DN, 409-D9) for 340B claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 - 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly warns that applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is considered fraudulent billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 - 2025-10-01], a detail not found in the provided policy excerpts.

kept on file at the pharmacy.

pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.

policy.text:
FFS Billing:
• Payment for the drug’s administration will be made through the outpatient Ambulatory Patient Groups
(APG) payment when administered in a clinic setting or, if administered on an inpatient basis, the All
Patient Refined Diagnosis Related Groups (APR-DRG) payment.
• Facilities administering ZOLGENSMA® will be reimbursed for the cost of the drug using the ordered
ambulatory fee schedule. The ordered ambulatory claim should be submitted on paper (using the
eMedNY 150003 claim form) and should include the facility’s actual acquisition cost by invoice.
Documentation of medical necessity that includes the criteria listed above must accompany the
claim. Ordered ambulatory billing guidelines can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
OrderedAmbulatory/PDFS/OrderedAmbulatory_Billing_Guidelines.pdf
• The following documentation must be included with the claim:
Manufacturer’s invoice showing the acquisition cost of the biologic, including all discounts, rebates
or incentives;
Documentation of the medication administration; and
Documentation of the criteria listed under NYS FFS Coverage Policy.

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, which has an effective date of January 1, 2022. The provider manual clarifies that home infusion drugs are not covered by a bundled payment and must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit, while policy documents discuss general drug administration coverage and pharmacy billing responsibilities.
answers.policy_definition: New York State Medicaid policy covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods when provided for FDA-approved indications and recognized off-label indications listed in drug compendia [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — February 01, 2019]. Payment for drug administration in a clinic setting is made through Ambulatory Patient Groups (APG) and for inpatient administration through All Patient Refined Diagnosis Related Groups (APR-DRG) [New York State Medicaid Update November 2019 Volume 35 Number 11:5 — November 01, 2019]. It is the pharmacy's responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, such as the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020]. For Fee-for-Service (FFS) pharmacy coverage and policy questions, providers should contact the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy Policy Unit [New York State Medicaid Update January 2022 Volume 38 Number 1:13 — January 01, 2022].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The New York State Medicaid Program does not offer a bundled payment for home infusion treatments, which include drugs, supplies, and services. Instead, home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — October 01, 2025]. Drugs, including home infusion drugs, that require administration by a practitioner can be covered under both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit, provided they meet specific criteria such as being billable in the NCPDP format [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — October 01, 2025]. Providers are responsible for determining if a patient has other insurance coverage and if that carrier covers the billed service [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — July 23, 2025].
similarities: Both sources indicate that drugs requiring administration by a healthcare provider, including home infusion drugs, are covered by Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — February 01, 2019], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources emphasize the provider's responsibility in billing, with policy stating the pharmacy's responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020] and the provider manual stating the provider's responsibility to determine other insurance coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — July 23, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that home infusion treatments are not covered by a bundled payment and must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — October 01, 2025], a detail not specified in the policy documents.

Policy documents discuss payment mechanisms for drug administration in clinic or inpatient settings via APG or APR-DRG [New York State Medicaid Update November 2019 Volume 35 Number 11:5 — November 01, 2019], while the provider manual focuses on the billing of home infusion drugs as pharmacy or medical benefits and the criteria for pharmacy benefit coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — October 01, 2025], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — October 01, 2025].

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document (December 1, 2024) and provides more detailed guidance on home infusion drug coverage and billing responsibilities. According to the provider manual, home infusion drugs and supplies are not covered by a bundled payment and must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit, with the provider responsible for determining other insurance coverage. Policy documents indicate that pharmacies are responsible for timely billing to the appropriate party.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source dated December 1, 2024, questions regarding NYS Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) drug coverage and policy should be directed to the Office of Health Insurance Programs Division of Program Development and Management, while FFS billing and claim questions should go to the eMedNY Call Center [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]. Earlier policy guidance from July 2020 states that it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, such as the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]. Drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods are covered when provided for FDA-approved indications and recognized off-label indications listed in drug compendia [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — Feb 01, 2019].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, the New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments, meaning home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025]. Certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider can be covered under both the medical and pharmacy benefits, provided they meet criteria such as being billable in NCPDP format [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025]. The provider is responsible for determining if a patient has other insurance coverage and if that carrier covers the service being billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — Jul 23, 2025].
similarities: Both sources indicate that drugs requiring administration by a practitioner, including home infusion drugs, are covered by NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — Feb 01, 2019]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources imply that pharmacies have a role in billing for drugs, with policy stating it's the pharmacy's responsibility to bill timely [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] and the provider manual discussing billing home infusion drugs as a pharmacy benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that there is no bundled payment for home infusion treatments and that drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], a detail not found in the provided policy documents.

The provider manual places responsibility on the provider to determine other insurance coverage for the patient and the service being billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — Jul 23, 2025], while policy documents state it is the pharmacy's responsibility to bill to the appropriate party, including commercial plans or Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020].

answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source dated December 1, 2024, questions regarding NYS Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) drug coverage and policy should be directed to the Office of Health Insurance Programs Division of Program Development and Management [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]. An earlier policy update from July 2020 states that it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, such as the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]. Drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods are covered when provided for FDA-approved indications and recognized off-label indications listed in drug compendia [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — Feb 01, 2019].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, the New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments, meaning home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025]. Certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider can be covered under both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit, provided they meet criteria such as being billable in NCPDP format [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025]. The provider is responsible for determining if the patient has other insurance coverage and if that carrier covers the service being billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — Jul 23, 2025].
similarities: Both sources indicate that drugs, including home infusion drugs, are covered under Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — Feb 01, 2019], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources imply the need for providers to understand billing responsibilities and appropriate parties for claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — Jul 23, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that there is no bundled payment for home infusion treatments and that drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], a detail not present in the policy documents.

The provider manual specifies that the provider is responsible for determining other insurance coverage for the patient [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — Jul 23, 2025], while policy documents state it is the pharmacy's responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party (e.g., commercial plan, Medicare, Medicaid) [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020].

seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.

plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny

policy.text:
FFS Billing:
• Payment for the drug’s administration will be made through the outpatient Ambulatory Patient Groups
(APG) payment when administered in a clinic setting or, if administered on an inpatient basis, the All
Patient Refined Diagnosis Related Groups (APR-DRG) payment.
• Facilities administering ZOLGENSMA® will be reimbursed for the cost of the drug using the ordered
ambulatory fee schedule. The ordered ambulatory claim should be submitted on paper (using the
eMedNY 150003 claim form) and should include the facility’s actual acquisition cost by invoice.

OrderedAmbulatory/PDFS/OrderedAmbulatory_Billing_Guidelines.pdf

or incentives;

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document (December 1, 2024) and provides more detailed guidance on home infusion drug coverage and billing responsibilities. According to the provider manual, home infusion drugs and supplies are not covered by a bundled payment and must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit, with the provider responsible for determining other insurance coverage. Policy documents indicate that pharmacies are responsible for timely billing to the appropriate party.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source dated December 1, 2024, questions regarding NYS Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) drug coverage and policy should be directed to the Office of Health Insurance Programs Division of Program Development and Management, and FFS billing and claim questions to the eMedNY Call Center [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]. Earlier policy guidance from July 2020 states that it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, such as the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]. Drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods are covered for FDA-approved indications and recognized off-label indications listed in drug compendia [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — Feb 01, 2019]. Payment for drug administration in a clinic setting is made through outpatient Ambulatory Patient Groups (APG) payment, or All Patient Refined Diagnosis Related Groups (APR-DRG) payment if inpatient [New York State Medicaid Update November 2019 Volume 35 Number 11:5 — Nov 01, 2019].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, the New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments, meaning drugs, supplies, and services must be billed separately as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025]. Home infusion drugs requiring administration by a health care provider can be covered under both medical and pharmacy benefits, provided they meet criteria such as being billable in NCPDP format and having a National Drug Code (NDC) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025]. The provider is responsible for determining if a patient has other insurance coverage and if that carrier covers the service being billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — Jul 23, 2025].
similarities: Both sources indicate that drugs, including home infusion drugs, are covered under specific conditions, such as FDA-approved indications [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — Feb 01, 2019] and eligibility for coverage under pharmacy or medical benefits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources imply the responsibility of the provider (pharmacy or facility) in the billing process, whether it's timely billing to the appropriate party [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] or determining other insurance coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — Jul 23, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that there is no bundled payment for home infusion treatments, requiring drugs and supplies to be billed as pharmacy or medical benefits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], a detail not specified in the policy documents.

Policy documents detail payment mechanisms for drug administration in clinic or inpatient settings via APG or APR-DRG payments [New York State Medicaid Update November 2019 Volume 35 Number 11:5 — Nov 01, 2019], which is not covered in the provided provider manual excerpts regarding home infusion.

The provider manual outlines specific criteria for drugs to be eligible for pharmacy benefit coverage, such as being billable in NCPDP format and having an NDC [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025], which is not present in the policy excerpts.

policy.text:
May 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Reminder: Substance Use Disorder Drug Treatment:
Medicaid Managed Care and Fee-for-Service Coverage
Important Legislation Regarding Drugs to Treat Substance Use Disorders:
• Medicaid policy requires Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) to provide
at least five days’ coverage for emergencies, without prior authorization, for medications used to
treat substance use disorders. This includes medication associated with the management of opioid
withdrawal and/or stabilization as well as medication used for opioid overdose reversal.
• As published in the August 2016 issue of the Medicaid Update, New York State Social Services Law
§364-j and Public Health Law §273, prohibit prior authorization under Medicaid FFS and MMC for
initial or renewal prescriptions for preferred or formulary forms of buprenorphine or injectable
naltrexone when used for detoxification or maintenance treatment of opioid addiction. The
frequency, quantity, and/or duration limits supported by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and
Compendia may continue to be applied.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2020 Volume 36 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2022
Volume 38 | Number 14
Opioid Overdose Prevention
The New York State (NYS) Department of Health (DOH) has recently released two brochures on Naloxone,
the life-saving medication that can reverse opioid overdose, for individuals who are pregnant or who’ve
recently given birth, as well as their support person(s):
• Patient education brochure tilted Do You or Someone You Know Take Opioids?
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2022 Volume 38 Number 14
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
February 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 4

Reminder: Pharmacy Coverage Includes Over the Counter Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner
of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued
naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found in the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) Non-Patient Specific Prescription for Naloxone with Pharmacy Dispensing
Protocol document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention
/docs/naloxone_standing_order_pharmacies.pdf.
To comply with NYS laws, regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, a pharmacy must submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Substance Use Disorder Drug Treatment,
Medicaid Managed Care and Fee-for-Service Coverage
Social Services Law §364-j and Public Health Law §273 prohibits prior authorization under Medicaid fee-for-
service (FFS) and Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) for initial or renewal prescriptions for preferred or formulary
forms of buprenorphine or injectable naltrexone when used for detoxification or maintenance treatment of opioid
addiction. The Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and Compendia-supported frequency quantity and/or
duration limits may continue to be applied.
The New York State (NYS) MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center
The NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center is a website that assists providers in identifying which
medications are included on the NYS MMC formularies, and can be found at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/. This includes a comprehensive listing of “Drugs to Treat
Chemical Dependence” showing formulary coverage by plan. To find this list, select “DRUG QUICKLISTS” from
the page headings, then select the “Therapeutic Classes, Other” tab whereupon a report can be customized to
show coverage by all or only select plans.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• The NYRx provides reimbursement for prescription drugs included on the NY Medicaid Pharmacy List of
Reimbursable Drugs, which can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
• Prescriptions must be dispensed and billed by a Medicaid enrolled pharmacy, using the member’s individual
Medicaid Client Identification Number (CIN).
Smoking Cessation Policy
• Smoking cessation therapy consists of certain prescription and non-prescription agents.
• Some smoking cessation therapies may be used together. Professional judgment should be exercised when
dispensing multiple smoking cessation products.
• For all smoking cessation products, the member must have a prescription or fiscal order.
• NY Medicaid reimburses for over-the-counter nicotine patches included on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
Emergency Contraception Drug Policy
Both prescription and OTC Emergency Contraception are a covered benefit for all Medicaid members without age
restriction. This includes individuals enrolled in the Family Planning Benefit Program.
